131 Avenue A "Wilson's Mission on Avenue A"

MLA BIBLIOGRAPHY & LYRICAL PHRASE EXPLANATIONS


[8] "One-three-one Avenue A, where the mission stood / Built in nineteen-twenty, brick and stone and wood" → Anchored in the Oasis Map construction date: "Was built in 1920." Unknown Author. Oasis Map, p. 1.


[13] "Beaux-Arts gold façade, ionic pilasters rise" → Drawn directly from the architectural survey: "Architecture Style: Beaux-Arts... Façade Color: Gold Fusion – 231 198 157... Window Detail: Ionic Pilasters." JG. (1883) Tompkins Square Park, p. 1.


[6] "Six stories cutting through the Lower East Side skies" → Pulled from the 1916 Atlas entry: "a 6 story brick building with saloon." G W Bromley & Co. 1916 Atlas of the Borough of Manhattan, p. 39.


[9] "Wilson's mission, gather every girl off the street / Barefoot, ragged children, giving them a seat" → Directly inspired by: "The children gathered here were for the most part barefooted, ragged street children, obliged to beg their daily bread." Richmond. (1872) New York Institutions 1609–1872, p. 494.


[9] "Sewing, reading, dinner — three cents feeds them all" → Drawn from: "All are furnished with a simple but good dinner...which the economical matron manages to supply at the rate of three cents per child." Richmond. (1872) New York Institutions 1609–1872, p. 494.


[11] "One-three-one Avenue A — they answered when you'd call" → Rooted in the institutional address and mission described: "Wilson Industrial School and Mission. 127 and 129 Avenue A." Hardy. (1870) Manual of the Corporation of NYC, p. 506.


[4] "Eighteen-fifty-three, a brick store marks this land" → References the 1853 Perris Maps: "a

Brick or stone store." Perris. 1853 Perris Maps, p. 48.


[5] "By eighty-five Robinson wrote 'Wilson Mission' — grand" → Drawn from: "In the 1885 Robinson Atlas...there is written 'Wilsons Mission House' as well as reference to 1 Structure; a Brick Building." Robinson. 1885 Robinson Atlas, p. 11.

[7] "Eighteen-ninety-seven, chapel and the hall" → Pulled from: "In the 1897 Bromleys Atlas...there is written 'Wilson Mission Ho., Chapel' as well as reference to 2 Structures." Bromley. 1897 Bromleys Atlas, p. 17.


[6] "Nineteen-sixteen, a six-floor saloon stands tall" → References: "In the 1916 Atlas of the Borough of Manhattan...a 6 story brick building with saloon." G W Bromley & Co. 1916 Atlas of the Borough of Manhattan, p. 39.


  1. "No structure in the sixty-seven Dripps Plan" → Directly from: "In the 1867 Dripps Plan, on

    131 Avenue A, there is reference to 0 structures." Dripps. 1867 Dripps Plan, p. 7.


    [10] "Then Wilson's ladies came and raised this holy stand" → Inspired by: "The efforts of these Christian ladies, in going to the very lowest sinks of society, seeking with all the sanctified arts of kindness and culture." Richmond. (1872) New York Institutions 1609–1872, p. 496.


    [19] "Flea Market Café by nineteen-eighty's turn" → Drawn from: "French Bistros Flea Market

    Cafe (131 Ave A)." Frank. (1980) Where to Find It, Buy It, Eat It In New York, p. 62.


    [16] "Bottle Squad at four a.m. — watch the corner burn" → Directly inspired by: "Bottle Squad,

    4:00 a.m., Ave. A & St. Marks, 1991." Patterson. (2007) Resistance, p. 278.


  2. "Nineteen-thirty land book, six floors and a store" → Pulled from: "In the 1930 Land Book of the Borough of Manhattan...a 6 story Building with Store." Bromley. 1930 Land Book of the Borough of Manhattan, p. 38.


  3. "Nineteen-fifty-five concrete — they built it to the core" → Echoes: "In the 1955 Manhattan Land Book...6 Story Brick or Stone or Concrete building with Store." Bromley. 1955 Manhattan Land Book, p. 28.


[13] "One-three-one Avenue A — Manhattan's layered lore" → A closing synthesis referencing the full architectural identity documented: "Architecture Style: Beaux-Arts...Façade Material: Brick." JG. (1883) Tompkins Square Park, p. 1.
